introduction.content:
Look into a mirror. You aren't seeing yourself as you are right now; you are seeing yourself as you were about 3 nanoseconds ago. That is how long it took light to bounce off your face and reach your eyes. If you look at the Moon, you see it as it was 1.3 seconds ago. If you look at the Sun, you see it as it was about 8 minutes ago.

In astronomy, looking outward into space is identical to looking backward in time. If we look far enough — past the stars of the Milky Way, past nearby galaxies like Andromeda, past distant clusters billions of light-years away — we can eventually see the universe as it was in its infancy, just hundreds of millions of years after it began.

This is the domain of **Cosmology**: the study of the universe as a single, evolving entity. It asks the largest questions humans have ever formulated: How old is the universe? How big is it? What is it made of? And perhaps most profoundly — how will it end? In this chapter, we will examine the evidence that shows our universe began in a hot, dense state 13.8 billion years ago, and discover why 95% of the cosmos is made of substances we can barely detect, let alone understand.

sections.section_title: The Big Bang: Not An Explosion In Space
sections.content:
The most widespread misconception about the Big Bang is the mental image of a tiny dot floating in empty black space that suddenly explodes, sending fragments (stars and galaxies) flying outward into the surrounding void.

This picture is wrong in a fundamental way.

The Big Bang was not an explosion *in* space. It was an explosion *of* space itself. There was no pre-existing void for the universe to expand into. Space — along with time, matter, and energy — was created in the event. Every point in space was the center of the expansion. This is why today, every galaxy in the universe observes every other galaxy moving away from it: it is not because they are flying through space from a central point of detonation, but because the space between them is stretching.

We accept this theory not because it sounds compelling, but because three independent pillars of hard evidence make it scientifically unavoidable.

content_block.title: The Three Pillars of Big Bang Evidence

items.name: 1. Cosmic Expansion (Hubble's Law)
items.description: Galaxies are moving away from us — and the further they are, the faster they recede.
characteristics: First measured by Edwin Hubble in 1929 using Cepheid variable stars as distance markers.
The relationship is linear: a galaxy twice as far moves away twice as fast.
Running the expansion backward in time implies that everything was once concentrated at one point — the Big Bang.

items.name: 2. The Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB)
items.description: The afterglow radiation of the Big Bang, arriving from every direction in the sky.
characteristics: Discovered accidentally in 1964–1965 by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson at Bell Labs, who won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1978 for this discovery.

The CMB is faint microwave radiation with a uniform temperature of ~2.7 K, filling the entire sky equally. As shown in Image 19.2, the ESA Planck satellite's all-sky CMB map reveals the tiny temperature fluctuations — just 1 part in 100,000 — in this afterglow radiation. The pattern of warmer and cooler patches represents density variations in the infant universe that eventually seeded the formation of all galaxies and large-scale structure.

It represents light released when the universe was just 380,000 years old and had cooled to ~3,000 K — the moment atoms first became stable. This light has been stretched by cosmic expansion ever since, cooling from ~3,000 K to just 2.7 K today. As shown in Image 19.2, the ESA Planck satellite's all-sky CMB map reveals the tiny temperature fluctuations — just 1 part in 100,000 — in this afterglow radiation. The pattern of warmer and cooler patches represents density variations in the infant universe that eventually seeded the formation of all galaxies and large-scale structure.

items.name: 3. Primordial Abundance of Elements
items.description: The chemical composition of the oldest, most pristine gas clouds in the universe matches Big Bang predictions precisely.
characteristics: The Big Bang model predicts the universe should be approximately 75% hydrogen and 25% helium by mass (or roughly 92% hydrogen atoms and 8% helium atoms by number — the distinction matters when comparing sources).
When astronomers examine the oldest, most metal-poor gas clouds and ancient stars, this is precisely what they find.
The quantities of deuterium, helium-3, and lithium-7 also match Big Bang Nucleosynthesis predictions — a remarkable multiple confirmation.

images.context: As you can see in Image 19.2, this all-sky CMB temperature map from the ESA Planck satellite shows the tiny temperature fluctuations — just 1 part in 100,000 — in the afterglow radiation from 380,000 years after the Big Bang. These ripples are the seeds that eventually grew into galaxies and galaxy clusters.

sections.content:
In 1929, Edwin Hubble observed that virtually every galaxy outside our Local Group is moving away from us. More importantly, he quantified a relationship: a galaxy twice as far away recedes twice as fast. This is **Hubble's Law**.

This motion is fundamentally different from objects moving *through* space. It is space itself that is expanding — carrying galaxies along with it like raisins in rising bread dough. The light traveling through this expanding space is being stretched along with the space it travels through. When a photon that was originally emitted as blue light travels through billions of light-years of expanding space, its wavelength stretches out, shifting it toward the red end of the spectrum. This is called **Cosmological Redshift** — distinct from the Doppler redshift caused by motion through space. As shown in Image 19.5, a photon emitted as a compact blue wave from a distant galaxy is progressively stretched as the space it travels through expands, arriving at Earth as a longer, redder wave — its wavelength increased in direct proportion to how much the universe expanded during the photon's journey.

content_block.why_it_happens: As a photon of light travels through the universe, the space it is traveling through expands. This stretches the photon's wavelength proportionally — shifting it toward longer, redder wavelengths, regardless of the speed of the emitting galaxy.
observable_effects: Distant galaxies appear redder than they actually are at their source.
The 'redshift value' (z) of a galaxy tells us how much the universe has expanded since that light was emitted. A z=1 galaxy's light was emitted when the universe was half its current size.
The CMB itself is the most extreme example: light emitted at ~3,000 K has been redshifted all the way down to ~2.7 K microwave radiation over 13.8 billion years of expansion.
content_block.real_world_example: Draw a wave on a rubber band and then stretch the rubber band. The wave gets longer — not because the wave itself moved, but because the medium it exists on expanded. This is exactly what expanding space does to photons of light. Image 19.8 uses the raisin bread analogy to make this concrete at cosmological scales: as the dough (space) rises, every raisin (galaxy) moves away from every other raisin — with no single raisin serving as the special center. An observer on any raisin would see all others receding, exactly as we observe galaxies receding from the Milky Way.

images.context: As you can see in Image 19.5, this three-panel diagram shows a photon being emitted as a compact blue wave from a distant galaxy, then stretching progressively as the space between galaxies expands, and finally arriving at Earth as a longer red wave — cosmological redshift illustrated step by step.

As you can see in Image 19.8, this raisin bread analogy shows a small loaf of dough (early universe) with raisins close together, then an expanded loaf (today) with all raisins further apart — demonstrating that every raisin sees every other raisin moving away, and none is the special 'center' of expansion.

sections.content: We can express Hubble's observation with a precise, simple formula. If we know the **Hubble Constant (H₀)** — the current rate of cosmic expansion — we can calculate how fast any galaxy should be receding based on its distance.

components.means: Recession velocity (speed the galaxy is moving away)

example.given: A galaxy is located 100 Megaparsecs (Mpc) away. H₀ is approximately 70 km/s/Mpc.
example.result: The galaxy is receding from us at 7,000 km/s — about 2.3% of the speed of light.

content_block.note: The Hubble Constant is currently one of the most actively debated values in all of cosmology — the so-called 'Hubble Tension.' CMB-based measurements (Planck satellite, 2018) give H₀ ≈ 67 km/s/Mpc, while local distance-ladder measurements (Cepheid variables and Type Ia supernovae) consistently give H₀ ≈ 73–74 km/s/Mpc. The discrepancy is statistically significant (above 5σ as of 2023–2024) and may require new physics beyond the standard cosmological model to resolve. This is one of the most exciting open problems in modern astronomy.

sections.section_title: The Dark Universe: What Is the Universe Made Of?
sections.content:
Here is the most humbling pie chart in science — and Image 19.6 renders it with full visual impact. If you add up every star, every planet, every gas cloud, every atom in your body — everything made of protons, neutrons, and electrons — you account for only **5%** of the total energy content of the universe. The pie is dominated by two components we cannot see, touch, or yet fully explain.

The remaining 95% is composed of two mysterious entities we cannot directly detect, interact with through electromagnetism, or yet fully explain.

content_block.title: The Full Composition of the Universe (Planck 2018)
rows: A mysterious energy of empty space that causes the expansion of the universe to accelerate. Its nature is almost entirely unknown — it may be Einstein's cosmological constant, or something more exotic.

Invisible matter that provides extra gravitational force — holding galaxies and galaxy clusters together, and forming the scaffolding of the Cosmic Web. Makes up ~85% of all matter, but only 27% of the total universe.
Every atom, molecule, star, planet, and living thing. The only component we can directly see, touch, and study — yet the clear minority of the universe's content.

images.context: As you can see in Image 19.6, this pie chart shows the 68/27/5 split of the universe's total energy budget — dramatically illustrating that the familiar matter composing stars, planets, and people is a small minority of what actually exists.

sections.content: The history of the universe is a story of gradual cooling and increasing complexity. Image 19.1 presents this entire 13.8-billion-year narrative as a single expanding cone — starting from a bright singular point at left (the Big Bang) and widening to the full observable universe at right (today), with each major milestone marked along the timeline. It began as an unimaginably hot, dense state, and as space expanded, it cooled down — allowing progressively more complex structures to form. We can trace this history through the following distinct eras.

content_block.intro: From the Big Bang to Now

items.title: The Planck Era (t < 10⁻⁴³ seconds)
items.description: The universe exists as a singularity of effectively infinite density and temperature. The four fundamental forces of nature are unified into one. Our current physics completely breaks down at these scales — we have no theory capable of describing this moment.
items.tip: Quantum gravity is needed to describe this era — a theory we don't yet have.

items.title: Inflation (t ≈ 10⁻³⁵ seconds)
items.description: Space undergoes exponential expansion in a tiny fraction of a second. The region that would eventually become our entire observable universe grew from sub-atomic scales to roughly the size of a grapefruit — note that this refers to the patch of space that became our observable universe, not the entire universe, which likely expanded far more. This rapid inflation smoothed out the universe to near-perfect uniformity and amplified tiny quantum fluctuations into the seeds that would eventually grow into galaxies.
items.tip: Inflation explains why the universe appears so uniform in temperature and density across the sky — distant regions that appear 'unconnected' were once in contact before being inflated apart.

items.title: Recombination (t = 380,000 years)
items.description: The universe has cooled to approximately 3,000 K — cool enough for electrons to combine with protons and form stable hydrogen atoms for the first time. With electrons no longer free to scatter photons, the universe becomes transparent. Light travels freely for the first time.
items.tip: This released light is the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) we detect today — the oldest light in the universe, now cooled to 2.7 K by 13.8 billion years of expansion. The pattern of density fluctuations at this moment — only 1 part in 100,000 — is exactly what we see mapped in Image 19.2 as the CMB temperature variations detected by the Planck satellite.

items.title: The Cosmic Dark Ages and First Stars (t = 200–400 million years)
items.description: After recombination, the universe enters a period astronomers call the 'Cosmic Dark Ages' — a cosmological term meaning the universe was literally dark, containing no stars or other light sources. Just cooling neutral hydrogen gas and invisible dark matter slowly gathering under gravity. Eventually, gravity concentrates enough gas to form the first stars, which begin to ionize the surrounding gas and light up the universe.
items.tip: JWST is now detecting some of these earliest galaxies, finding they formed more quickly and vigorously than models predicted.

items.title: Dark Energy Dominates (t ≈ 9 billion years — ongoing)
items.description: For the first several billion years, gravity was the dominant force shaping the universe's expansion — slowing it down. But as the universe grew and matter became more dilute, dark energy — a constant energy density of empty space — gradually took over. About 5 billion years ago, the expansion of the universe stopped decelerating and began to accelerate. We are living in this dark-energy-dominated era right now.
items.tip: The ultimate fate of the universe — a cold, dark, ever-expanding void — is driven by this dark energy dominance.

content_block.example: If the entire history of the universe were compressed into one calendar year: the Big Bang is January 1st, the first stars appear in late January, our solar system forms in early September, the first life on Earth appears in late September, and all of recorded human history occupies the last 15 seconds of December 31st.

images.context: As you can see in Image 19.1, this expanding cone timeline diagram maps the entire 13.8-billion-year history of the universe from left (Big Bang, a single point) to right (today, the full observable universe), with key events — inflation, recombination, first stars, galaxy formation, and dark energy dominance — marked at their correct positions along the timeline.

concepts.explanation: The moment 13.8 billion years ago when the universe began expanding from an extremely hot, dense state. It was an expansion of space itself — not an explosion in space — so there was no single central point and no pre-existing void it expanded into.

A mysterious energy of empty space that makes up 68% of the universe's total energy content and is driving the accelerating expansion of space. Its ultimate nature remains one of the deepest unsolved problems in physics.

The 'afterglow' of the Big Bang — thermal radiation released 380,000 years after the universe began, when it first became transparent. Now cooled to 2.7 K by cosmic expansion, it permeates the entire sky uniformly and is one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the Big Bang.

The spherical region of the universe from which light has had enough time (13.8 billion years) to reach us — approximately 93 billion light-years in diameter (larger than 2 × 13.8 billion light-years because space has been expanding throughout the journey).

ideas: The universe had a definite beginning 13.8 billion years ago — it is not eternal and unchanging.
We know the Big Bang happened because galaxies are moving apart (expansion), we can detect the leftover heat (CMB), and the chemical composition of the oldest gas matches predictions precisely.
Space itself is expanding, carrying galaxies along with it — there is no center and no edge to the expansion.

The vast majority of the universe (95%) is composed of Dark Matter and Dark Energy — substances that remain deeply mysterious and are among the most active areas of research in modern physics and cosmology.

activities.title: See the CMB — Visually
activities.description: View the actual CMB map from the ESA Planck satellite and explore the oldest light in the universe.
instructions: Visit the ESA Planck satellite image gallery at: https://www.esa.int/Science_Exploration/Space_Science/Planck
Open the full-sky CMB temperature map. The oval shape shows the entire sky in one projection, like a flattened globe.
The colors represent tiny temperature differences — the blue patches are 0.0001°C cooler than average; red patches are 0.0001°C warmer. These fluctuations were only 1 part in 100,000.

These temperature differences represent slight density variations in the infant universe — regions that were slightly denser eventually became galaxy clusters; slightly less dense regions became voids.

Note: The classic 'hear the Big Bang on a radio' activity worked with analog TV static before the digital broadcasting switchover (completed in most countries around 2009–2012). Modern digitally-tuned devices do not capture CMB noise. The Planck map is a far more accurate way to experience this discovery.

next_chapter.preview: We know how the universe began, but are we alone in it? In the next chapter, we will explore the hunt for planets orbiting other stars and the scientific search for signs of life beyond Earth.
